title: Samantha Olinger -Operations Interview | Engagement Manager (Account Management)
meeting_title: Samantha Olinger -Operations Interview | Engagement Manager (Account Management)

transcript.text: Thank you.
Hi, Samantha.
Hi, Bridget.
Nice to meet you.
You too.
How are you?
Good, good.
How about yourself?
I'm not bad.
I can't complain today.
Well, it is almost the end of the week too, right?
I know.
Yep, the end is nearing.
Yes.
Awesome.
I was just pulling up some of the notes from your call with Andy.
And was looking at your resume, but it's very nice to meet you.
I run our ops teams here, so I've been here for coming up on a year and run our finance and HR and recruiting and teams.
And I work closely with Andy on customer delivery stuff as well.
Okay, good.
Yeah, I know.
I think my first interview, they said that you're one of the longest employees there.
Yeah, probably.
I think I was in the first 10 or so.
Oh, goodness.
Yeah, it's a lot of new...
Everything is fresh here.
New clients, new people, new business, but I like it.
That's what I was looking for when I joined.
was like, I'm ready to be somewhere that's like kind of blank slate where you can, everything needs to be kind of designed from scratch.
And so you can think about it like very first principles, what actually is needed.
Right.
Yeah.
Instead of like coming in and being like, hey, you know, we've done this for a while.
Can we change it?
Yeah, exactly.
Cool.
Well, I have like three questions I try to go through with everybody.
So I'd love to dive into those.
And then we have time at the end, we can do some questions for you as well.
But to start, I just love to talk about like a renewal or upsell that you're very proud of.
Maybe one of the, I don't know, more notable ones in your career.
And just tell me about like, what was the situation?
Maybe it was a bit more complicated.
How did you go about getting it to an end result, even if it was not a renewal or an upsell?
But even if there was no success, let's call it.
Tell me about a dicey one you've dealt with.
Okay.
Well, I am going to choose a successful one because I think that this one is probably one of the most difficult that I have ever gone through.
My day-to-day point of contact was 100% a champ, right?
We work on the regular.
We had just gone through a site migration in that year.
They had also acquired about four different businesses that we had merged into the new website.
So we had done a lot of work.
We had a lot of progress.
We had so many wins to show.
So I went into this and I was like, no question, this is a renewal, right?
Like, didn't think twice about it.
And I spoke with the, you know, leadership there and leadership.
Told me very quickly that they were planning on going in another direction.
And were you going into it like with a kind of renewal convo in mind or was it you just came across that in a random conversation?
no, it was a renewal conversation.
Yeah.
And, you know, I mean, I was going into like share our wins, share our progress, share even just those additional value points that we had seen throughout the year.
Right.
A lot of those things are very hard to accomplish, you know, but when you have a supportive team, you're able to do it.
So, like I said, I went in there, you know, deck stack.
was like, I have all this stuff.
There's no way they're going to tell me now.
And leadership was like, we decided to go in another direction.
And I was kind of floored.
And, you know, I was like, do you mind sharing?
Right.
I feel communication transparency is really, you know, the best way to approach any situation.
And I was fortunate enough that they were like, you know what, we're going to go with an agency so that way we can have a larger scope of work.
So I was like, okay, I completely understand.
Can you tell me some of the pain points that were harder to work through?
Because I feel like we've accomplished a lot as a team and everything.
And really their biggest takeaway was AI, to be honest.
They wanted to be able to be more present in mentions and citations.
They wanted to see more visibility there.
So I took that back to my team.
We talked with the product team.
We talked with even the support team.
So we had a product that we hadn't quite launched yet.
It was still in beta testing.
And we gave them the opportunity, if they would do a free three-month contract, to test it out.
And, you know, so kind of walk through workflow and work on it with us.
What do you mean workflow?
And this could be just my not knowing how the product worked in the first place.
So was that like something custom if they wanted to have AI visibility?
Or how does it, how did it work in the product and your offering?
In our offering.
So like I said, so this is one of the product was still in beta testing.
So we really didn't have like a structured like, Hey, how are we going to help you understand what this tool does?
So how can we show you where your visibility is and how to build on that visibility?
Right.
So we kind of gave them the opportunity to be our partner in establishing that initial, you know, playbook for how we were going to set those things up.
Okay.
And was it something like, would your team have to build something from scratch or would you just go get an, buy another tool that was just like an LLM visibility tool?
So we had been working on like our own LLM visibility tool.
Yeah.
So that's really awesome.
It just pulls in like where you're mentioned prompts, like a lot of the branded terms in there.
And then it would like filter some of the questions that were like cluster category related to that industry.
It was the industry was a landscaping industry, right?
So a lot of like, you know, seasonal terms like flowers and tree landscaping, things like that.
So what we did is this was about six months ago.
So we were coming into summer.
So we talked about, you know, like, hey, let's get some ideas on how we can use this tool to look at what's coming up for the winter season, snow removal, things like that, right?
And I also knew from working with them previously, snow removal has been a struggle for them.
So that's why I wanted to focus on it, see if we could get some extra visibility there before their site, before.
What kind of company is this?
They're a landscaping company.
Okay.
Yeah.
So and they do like concrete, like construct, I guess, construction landscaping.
So we went through that process.
Like I said, we've really built out like a playbook on how to research everything, you know, because they really didn't know either, you know.
So we use the tool to kind of dive into where.
their brand was popular at, where it was being mentioned at.
From there, we took some of those insights and saw how we could build out further.
And then, you know, of course, along the way, we tracked everything.
We tracked the terms we were using.
We tracked the pages that we were directing these terms to so we could set up dashboards for monitoring.

Then at the end of the three months, you know, we took it back to their leadership team and showed them that, hey, you know, not only did we, you know, work together as a team on a very tight timeline, but we were able to produce results for you.
And we gave you something prior to anybody seeing it, you know.
So, you know, we value you as a customer and we hope that you at this time value us for, you know, what we can bring to you and what we can offer.
So, you know, setting it up like that really did help them to see, you know, we've worked alongside of
For quite a few years.
So I think that kind of drew them back to, you know, yes, we want to be able to broaden our scope of work.
And then they decided to actually bring us on or keep us on and bring the agency on.
So now I get to work with the agency in that.
Oh, so what's how do they divide who does what between you and the agency?
So the agency does meet with us on our team.
The agency is hosting more of their paid media.
Right.
So we couple that with what we're doing organically.
So we're not overlapping one with the other, of course.
And, you know, so that way, too, if there is something, you know, that the paid media team wants to work on, we can always shift what we're doing.
So it's actually been really nice because it does give me, you know, a full idea of what we're doing.
And then also for that, you know, we can branch off and give the paid team additional ideas, too.
So what I'm like, in hindsight, looking back on the relationship.
What would you have done differently?
It seems like it came out of left field.
It definitely does.
And I think this is one where I was really reliant on my main point of contact, that they were keeping me in the loop there.
So we did ultimately have a conversation about transparency and things like that, because he had known that they were talking to the agency.
And it was like, it's OK to let us know things like that, right?
Because ultimately, we can either work with you at the agency, or I can help you even get prepared to transition.
If you're not staying with us, that's completely fine.
But I don't want you to go into a transition and not have any of your back data, not have any of the research that we've done, anything like that.
So either way, I was prepared to continue to support them.
You know, and I let him know that and he was like, OK, from now, you know, from now on, I can do that, you know.
know, because he's like, I didn't want to like lose you guys, but you know, it's ultimately not my decision.
And we also made the choice there too, that maybe, you know, his leadership needs to be included in at least like our quarterly reviews.

So they kind of have an idea of not just like, hey, these are the numbers, but like, these are the actual things that we have done and how long they've taken and how much value they're bringing back to your business.
Mm-hmm.
Anything else you would have done differently or that you do differently with other clients now because of this?
With other clients now?
Yeah, definitely.
I try to make sure that we have everyone on board.

Even if you're not attending sessions, I want to know that anytime we are working through something, you're either getting some sort of feedback from leadership, or you are, you know, incorporating them in some way, right?
Like not just.
It's going through the motions to go through the motions anymore, right?
Like this is a full team experience.
Awesome.
And then what, if you had to break it down to one thing, like what was your kind of biggest personal accomplishment in that experience that you were proud of?
So I've been at Brightish for almost four years now, and this was the first time I got to be included in a test group since I started.
So when I joined Bright Edge, it was smaller.
Like they had a couple of like playbooks and things like that.

So I have built some of those out, but not from like ground up with, you know, I mean, I've worked with product to enhance things that are already there, give them recommendations or feedback from customers, but never from like the starting phase to be like, hey, you know, we want to not only do we want to use this tool for research, but we also want to catch up.
Okay.
Thank
Make sure that we can see opportunities in here.
So that helped the product team.
Like we had some, you know, sessions where it was like, hey, these are, you know, my customers told me like opportunities are important.
So how can we see visible opportunities in here?
Is there a way to also scale the growth of what our opportunities are to make sure that we're at least making a dent in these things, right?
So being able to create a clear track of communication between the product team and the customer from that level, like the bottom level, was fantastic.
Awesome.

Shifting gears a little bit, how have you been able to, obviously now you've been at BrightEdge for quite a few years, but how, maybe initially, like how did you get yourself, how did you become, you know, knowledgeable enough and credible in like the SEO content world, just seeing
You came from a bit of a different background, right?

How did you get yourself up to speed so you can have conversations with whoever's on the client side and probably in an SEO role, I would imagine, on the client side and be able to have conversations with them on your own kind of thing?
Just curious how you went about that.
So I know my resume, if look back, I was a hairstylist for a really long time.
And I always kind of enjoyed the digital landscape then.
I would dabble in it, play, and it was more on the social media side, which is how I got into media event coordination.
But during that time, too, I also went and pursued my master's, which is in communication technology.
So I never stopped teaching and learning for myself.
When I was a hairstylist, I had my own website and I did everything on it.
And, you know, it was still fairly basic because I'll be honest, like coding isn't exactly my favorite thing and updating code and everything like that.
But.
But.
But.
But I know how to do it, right?
So I wanted to keep my skills fresh.
And then, of course, when I did media coordination, I did some of the website development there.
And then from there, I moved into an agency.
So, and it was very small, right?
So we had to all communicate and work really well together.
And one of the SEO leads there, he had come from another company.
He had like 10, 15 years of experience.
So we kind of, you know, really meshed well together.
So I learned a little bit more from him.
And that was actually probably what was able, how I was able to get the role at BrightEdge there.
But of course, just continuing my own personal education, right?

Like, anytime there's a new, you know, certification with Google, with SEMrush, even though they were a competitor, you know, like, it doesn't hurt to have additional sources there, as well as just keeping on top of things, you know, that come out with news and everything like that.
Um, how's it?
Been trying to keep up with the AEO, whatever you want to call it, GEO.
Whatever the acronym of the week is.
It has been so much fun, right?
I feel like it's chaotic, but I do really enjoy it.
I think because I feel like AEO and GEO truly shift towards that research perspective.
And it's so nice, right?
Because for years, SEO was all there was, and it was so broad, right?
Like you were focused on these terms that were so generic.
And now coming into this space, you're really getting down to those niche topics.
So I think it's, you know, so beneficial because you can, I feel like I can help people more now, right?
I can tell you that, hey, we're not just going to write a blog about healthcare.
We're going to write a blog about how this specific healthcare facility launched in this area.
And, you know, how it is.
Going to impact that community because that's what people are looking for, you know, and I think just keeping up on the education of it has just it's it's been good because it keeps you fresh.
It keeps you new.
It makes you change your perspective.
And, you know, sometimes in a digital role, you can be like, this is what works.
It's easy to stick with.
I know it's a quick win.
Or you can be like, listen, are you ready to do an A-B test and take a risk with me?
Because if you are, you are probably going to see some really good results.
Yeah, I like that.
It lets you go way deeper.
You're generally answering a particular question instead of just like a broad topic.
Yeah.
What kind of like just I know Marilia probably asked you this as well, but like what kind of what kind of ways do you use AI?
To be more efficient.
So, of course.
Just like everybody else, know, I've gone to the LLMs and be like, hey, so this is my least favorite thing to do.
I, you know, I do have a template for my agendas and follow-ups, but like, I mean, if I could just not send those, it would be great.
Which, no, I wasn't able to automate that through.
Like, I wasn't able to be like, just read my mind and put in here what you need to.
Um, however, you know, like being able to be like, hey, is there a better way to make sure that this information is being passed along?
Is there a more creative way to like format my agendas and my follow-ups so people are, you know, more engaged with them?
Uh, things like that.
So, of course, you know, or like, how can I streamline my workflow?
Or my Tuesdays are crazy.
Do you see a better way to structure them?
So I've done, of course, that basic stuff.
Um, but we do use, you know, some project management here that really helps to kind of keep you on top of things and kind of guide you to like, hey, little note, like this is coming up or that.
So it helps to like.
Automate that workflow.
And then other than that, that really, you know, the research portion of it, and that's just from, you know, the platform.
I've done some demos on other content creation and research, you know, AI research platforms just to see how they compare to BrightEdge, but that's really it.
Awesome.

What, tell me about some recent, or I guess it's not going be recent, tell me about some impactful feedback you've received, like both constructive feedback and something like very positive, you're doing well.
Okay.
So I think when it comes to, you know, positive feedback from my managers, I always hear that they are, you know, impressed.
They feel like with what it is my intuition.
However, when you really look at it, it's just that idea of being.
Proactive, right?
Because if you are ahead of the game, if you're keeping tabs on, you know, what's going on with your client, how scores, things like that, it's really easy to kind of project what's going to happen.
You know, there are a few of those blindsides, like I mentioned, but for the most part, it's kind of easy to see where things are going.
So my managers are always like, your intuition with customers is so great.
So I, like I said, I'll keep that behind the scenes, though.
what do you think, like, because having a pulse on health, health of an account is like, look, I also said this the other day, like, that is what makes the difference.
Like, what are your, how do you do it?
I know sometimes it's just like some people are naturally good at that and the relationship matters and stuff, too.
But is there anything like, could even just be how your personality is or like, how you interact with your clients that makes you.
Are able to just kind of have like a good gut instinct on what's going on?

I mean, I think it's a little bit of that, but also too, like I'm pretty meticulous in like monitoring those things and not just like going on and looking at like, oh, today at Gainsight told me, you know, that this person is only at, you know, like 50% usage, but like actually tracking what that means, right?
Right.
Because I think that's where it becomes noticeable, right?
So do they have like an ebb and flow throughout the year?
Are there times that they use it more, use it less?
So you're like, all right, I don't necessarily have to be concerned right now because it seems like at the end of every quarter when they're putting together reports, they tend to drop off, right?
So no big like red flag there.
So I do track that on my own.
But also too, I think when I'm in sessions, or when I get an email, like my first go to is, why would somebody send me this, right?
So, or why would you bring up this topic?
So I need to know if it is like
And if it is, that tells me that there is a point that you're struggling with.
And before it gets to the point that you're overwhelmed, we're going to tackle it.
So I think that that's kind of the key for me is just making sure that there's always a continuous discovery going on.
Yeah.
Yeah, that's kind of what I was thinking you're getting at was like, just like asking them, asking the question half the time, just like, what's going on?
Are you guys looking at any other vendors or cool new tools or, you know, and just as sometimes I just tell you and you got all your, you got the goldmine of info.
I don't know.
I feel like I try to be more, more sly about it too.
Like I'm never, like, I try not to be like the very like, oh, hey, you know, I'm always like, so I saw this cool thing.
And I'm like, did you see it?
Yeah.
Yeah.
That's a good one.
Yeah.
Or like, what, what kind of stuff is getting passed around?
Like your company or your leadership, like what kind of initiatives are they more interested in?
Tell me some constructive feedback or something you're working on in general.

Something that I have been working on in general, I think is, you know, I've never really seen myself as stepping into a leadership role, but like it's interested me a little bit more over the last couple of, I would say probably like the last year or so.
Um, just like seeing how things change and how I am able to assist others.
So I've been working on more of like that, um, you know, the ideas of like building out like programs, right.
For maybe not like those so common questions, right.
You have your questions that, you know, every customer is going to ask and we probably have 10,000 resources on them, right.
Here's a video, here's a one page or whatever.
Um, so anytime I get like a niche topic or something along those.
And then just being proactive about sharing it, right?
Because before it would be like, oh, I had this thing.
And then somebody would be like, oh, I had the same problem.
And you're like, no, I have to sort back through everything.
Is this, you know, like, or let me find this for you.
And then you, you read it.
Do you tell me, does that help you?
And then get feedback there.
Kind of just been more, you know, proactive in that, like, how can I share and how can I be more of a leader?
You know, I've been where I'm at for a while.
I've been in the digital space for a while.
So I do have a lot of, you know, things to share.
And even like what we just talked about with that insight, you know, it's sometimes it's hard to pinpoint it.
But at other points, you can be like, well, are you doing this?
You know, are you doing continuous discovery?
Are you being transparent with your customers, right?
Like, hey, this isn't working.
We need to do something else.
So.
Awesome.
I love that.
Yeah, always like the best way to go into leadership is just like start doing the things, especially in startups, right?
It's like.
And just start doing it.
And then, you know, when someone thinks of, oh, we need that role, it's like, oh, this person's already doing it.
So yeah.
Awesome.
Well, thank you for sharing all that.
I think that's kind of all my questions on my end.
Is there anything I can answer for you?
I don't know.
I think, I mean, everybody has answered a lot of what I've had.

You know, I know, have you worked with like a lot of, I'm guessing you guys don't have like a huge book yet, but have you seen like commonalities among customers or, you know, common issues that you guys have had so far with them?
Yeah.
Yeah.
It's so interesting because we're so new.
And so, and to date, a lot of our engagements have been more of like a service.
Like it feels like we're an agency, but really we've just been doing that too for cashflow.
And then on the side.
We have a 15-person engineering team that's building the platform that we're going to launch early next year.
And so it will be like a content.
You'll be able to see all the visibility in the tool, both LLM and SEO.
You'll be able to produce content, edit it, publish it all within one place.
But there's always going to be people who don't want to operate systems themselves.

And so we'll continue to have like two different delivery models, I guess I'll call it, or like distribution models where it's like maybe we have some clients who use the platform themselves and we help them use it.
Maybe there's others where we just fully operate it for them like a managed service, which is what we do today essentially.

So going into next year, we're going to all of a sudden have like different types of clients and then different, you know, there's a totally different definition of health when you're operating the product versus they are.
So anyway, all that to say.
Like so far, most of our, the commonalities are just like in this services world where I think a lot of like the challenges we've had is just services just really hard.

Like when people put you in the bucket of an agency, which we don't really consider ourselves that because we have like proprietary tech that we're using, but just because the engagement model today is like they're removed from it.
They can't see it.
I just think it like comes with this lesser value to it, right?
So the sooner we can open up the platform and show them all the stuff we've been building and doing and how it works behind the scene, I think they'll be easier.
So that's one thing.
And then the other thing is just like, it's, there's just clients who have like crazy expectations and we're getting way better at identifying that before in pre-sales.
Just like crazy expectations.
Like they don't seem, if it's.
Which is crazy because they're marketers, but they seem to think you're going to get results overnight with things.
Or they have like a crazy complicated review process for content.
Like we've had probably two or three where it's like, we literally have one where it's like a supplement company, like women's supplements.
And they have doctors who have to review the content.
it's like, okay, this is going to take forever to get that published.
Also like medical level reviewers on like, it is AI generated content.
It's edited by humans, but like, they're not doctors.
So just stuff like that.
We've just learned, okay, we have such a better idea of like, who are good to serve customers now.
And like, once they're excited to work with us, there's not a lot of friction.
They trust us to just kind of go for it.
They're there to review, but they're not going to block.
They understand that this is a long game.
So anyways, that's, I would say like the early.
Like, if we lost customers, it was early on, like, last April and May, not enough, like, expectations setting up front.
Okay.
All right.
Well, and I mean, I think, too, with this.
Are you still there?
Oh, there we go.
Yeah.
I think.
Okay.
I'll check.
Maybe.
Yeah, you're moving now.
My internet says unstable, so.
That's what I was going to say.
You froze, but I could still hear you.
Okay, good.
Okay.
I'll see if that helps.
There, I was going to say that's usually.
Okay.
Well, I do.
I should go anyways.
I have another meeting, but, um, yes.
Um, sorry about this connection.
Let me see if I can get my hotspot going.
It's okay.
One second.
Are you there?
There.
Are you, can you hear me now?
Okay.
I was just trying to type you in the chat.
I was going to be like, if you have another meeting, that is completely fine.
I've been there.
I totally understand.
So no.
Yeah, that's probably it though.
But yeah, absolutely.
Thank you for your time too today.
I really appreciate it.
You guys are so fast.
It's unreal.
Yeah.
Yeah.
She's on top of it.
She knows we're looking for headcount.
She's filling it.
Also soon enough, it becomes the holidays.
It's very hard to get on people's calendars.
So.
I know.
Yeah, that was.
Our initial conversation.
So I was like, oh, this is probably going to be a longer process than I thought.
No, if we can do something in two weeks, that's like, that's kind of the goal.
It's like, let's just get through it.
So let me talk, let me talk with Andy and Marilia after this, and we'll get back to you on next steps as well.
Sounds good.
Thank you so much.
Yeah.
Thanks.
Nice to meet you.
Nice to meet you too.
Bye.
Bye.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Interview Samantha Olinger for the Engagement Manager role.](https://fathom.video/share/oU7B4ZyfL2hkgugPSvwWqd1QZPbxkmgc?tab=summary&timestamp=293.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [**Saved a landscaping client from churn** by offering a 3-month beta test of a new LLM visibility tool, directly addressing their stated need for AI capabilities.](https://fathom.video/share/oU7B4ZyfL2hkgugPSvwWqd1QZPbxkmgc?tab=summary&timestamp=563.0)
  - [**Proactive account management prevents churn** by meticulously tracking usage patterns and asking strategic questions to uncover hidden client needs before they become problems.](https://fathom.video/share/oU7B4ZyfL2hkgugPSvwWqd1QZPbxkmgc?tab=summary&timestamp=1468.0)
  - [**Building a leadership track record** by creating and sharing resources for niche client problems, which positions Samantha as a go-to expert.](https://fathom.video/share/oU7B4ZyfL2hkgugPSvwWqd1QZPbxkmgc?tab=summary&timestamp=1625.0)
  - [**The company is transitioning** from a services-only model to a product-first one (platform launch early 2025), requiring new client health metrics and delivery models.](https://fathom.video/share/oU7B4ZyfL2hkgugPSvwWqd1QZPbxkmgc?tab=summary&timestamp=1783.0)

## Topics

### Client Renewal Case Study

  - [**Problem:** A landscaping client, despite recent project success (site migration, 4 acquisitions), planned to churn for an agency offering a broader scope.](https://fathom.video/share/oU7B4ZyfL2hkgugPSvwWqd1QZPbxkmgc?tab=summary&timestamp=434.0)
  - [**Root Cause:** The client's leadership wanted AI capabilities for brand mentions and citations, a need not communicated by the day-to-day contact.](https://fathom.video/share/oU7B4ZyfL2hkgugPSvwWqd1QZPbxkmgc?tab=summary&timestamp=544.0)
  - [**Solution:** Offered the client a free 3-month beta test of a new, unlaunched LLM visibility tool.](https://fathom.video/share/oU7B4ZyfL2hkgugPSvwWqd1QZPbxkmgc?tab=summary&timestamp=563.0)
      - [**Goal:** Partner with the client to build the tool's initial workflow and playbook.](https://fathom.video/share/oU7B4ZyfL2hkgugPSvwWqd1QZPbxkmgc?tab=summary&timestamp=588.0)
      - [**Focus:** Used the tool to research winter-specific terms (e.g., "snow removal"), a known client pain point.](https://fathom.video/share/oU7B4ZyfL2hkgugPSvwWqd1QZPbxkmgc?tab=summary&timestamp=668.0)
  - [**Outcome:** The client renewed, keeping Samantha's team for organic work and hiring the agency for paid media, creating a collaborative partnership.](https://fathom.video/share/oU7B4ZyfL2hkgugPSvwWqd1QZPbxkmgc?tab=summary&timestamp=781.0)

### Account Management Philosophy

  - [**Proactive Discovery:** Meticulously tracks client usage patterns to differentiate normal fluctuations from true red flags.](https://fathom.video/share/oU7B4ZyfL2hkgugPSvwWqd1QZPbxkmgc?tab=summary&timestamp=1504.0)
  - [**Strategic Questioning:** Asks "why" behind client topics to uncover underlying struggles before they escalate.](https://fathom.video/share/oU7B4ZyfL2hkgugPSvwWqd1QZPbxkmgc?tab=summary&timestamp=1546.0)
  - [**Leadership Development:** Building a track record by creating and sharing resources for niche client problems, positioning Samantha as an expert.](https://fathom.video/share/oU7B4ZyfL2hkgugPSvwWqd1QZPbxkmgc?tab=summary&timestamp=1625.0)

### Company Context & Challenges

  - [**Business Model Transition:** Shifting from a services-only model to a product-first one.](https://fathom.video/share/oU7B4ZyfL2hkgugPSvwWqd1QZPbxkmgc?tab=summary&timestamp=1783.0)
      - [**Product:** A new platform (LLM/SEO visibility, content creation) launches early 2025.](https://fathom.video/share/oU7B4ZyfL2hkgugPSvwWqd1QZPbxkmgc?tab=summary&timestamp=1783.0)
      - [**Future Delivery:** Will offer both self-serve platform access and managed services.](https://fathom.video/share/oU7B4ZyfL2hkgugPSvwWqd1QZPbxkmgc?tab=summary&timestamp=1821.0)
  - [**Current Challenges:**](https://fathom.video/share/oU7B4ZyfL2hkgugPSvwWqd1QZPbxkmgc?tab=summary&timestamp=1768.0)
      - [**Perceived Value:** The services-only model is often valued less than a product.](https://fathom.video/share/oU7B4ZyfL2hkgugPSvwWqd1QZPbxkmgc?tab=summary&timestamp=1860.0)
      - [**Expectation Setting:** Learning to identify and avoid clients with unrealistic expectations (e.g., overnight results, complex review processes).](https://fathom.video/share/oU7B4ZyfL2hkgugPSvwWqd1QZPbxkmgc?tab=summary&timestamp=1901.0)

## Next Steps

  - [**Bridget:** Discuss the interview with Andy and Marilia.](https://fathom.video/share/oU7B4ZyfL2hkgugPSvwWqd1QZPbxkmgc?tab=summary&timestamp=2110.0)
  - [**Bridget:** Follow up with Samantha on next steps.](https://fathom.video/share/oU7B4ZyfL2hkgugPSvwWqd1QZPbxkmgc?tab=summary&timestamp=2110.0)

action_items.description: Sync w/ Andy + Marilia re: Samantha; update Samantha on next steps
